definition: A graph is a collection of vertices (nodes) connected by edges. Edges can be directed or undirected, weighted or unweighted, and may form cycles — making graphs the most general ADT for modeling relationships.
layman: A road map is a graph: towns are dots, roads are lines. Some roads are one-way (directed), some have distances (weighted), and you can drive around a block (cycle).
content:
## Graphs

Formally a graph G = (V, E) is a set of **vertices** V and a set of **edges** E. Three independent dimensions:

| Dimension | Variants |
|-----------|----------|
| Direction | undirected, directed |
| Weight | unweighted, weighted |
| Cycles | acyclic (DAG), with cycles |

A **tree** is a special graph: connected, undirected, acyclic, with one parent per non-root node.

### Representations

Two common ways to store a graph:

- **Adjacency list** — `dict[Node, list[Node]]` (or list of edges). Best when the graph is sparse (E ≪ V²). O(V + E) space.
- **Adjacency matrix** — `V × V` matrix where `M[u][v] = 1` (or weight) iff edge exists. Best when graph is dense or you need O(1) edge existence checks. O(V²) space.

For weighted graphs the values become weights instead of booleans.

### Core algorithms

| Algorithm | Purpose | Complexity (adj-list) |
|-----------|---------|-----------------------|
| **BFS** | shortest path in unweighted graph, level-by-level traversal | O(V + E) |
| **DFS** | reachability, topological sort, cycle detection | O(V + E) |
| **Dijkstra** | shortest path with non-negative weights | O((V + E) log V) with a heap |
| **Topological sort** | linear order respecting dependencies (DAGs only) | O(V + E) |

### Why this matters for Gen AI

- **Knowledge graphs** — entities and relations are vertices and edges; graph-RAG uses them.
- **Tool / agent orchestration** — LangChain/LangGraph models agents as a graph of nodes (tools, decisions, output sinks). Cycles allow loops; DAGs allow safe scheduling.
- **Vector index internals** — HNSW (Hierarchical Navigable Small World) is literally a multi-layer graph; finding nearest neighbors is a graph search.
- **Citation / dependency graphs** — documents that cite each other, packages depending on others, RAG result clusters.
- **Conversation forks** — when an agent retries multiple branches, the history becomes a DAG of attempts.

Graphs are the most general structure in the basic-ADT family — trees, linked lists and even arrays are special cases.
keyPoints: Graph = vertices + edges; pick directed/undirected, weighted/unweighted, cyclic/acyclic per problem.
Adjacency list is the default representation; adjacency matrix for dense graphs or O(1) edge tests.
BFS uses a queue (unweighted shortest path); DFS uses a stack (reachability, cycle detection, topo-sort).
Dijkstra uses a priority queue and assumes non-negative weights.
Tools like HNSW (vector search) and LangGraph (agent orchestration) ARE graphs under the hood.
